abstract: To address the longstanding challenge of metal contamination in traditional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), we report a metal-free ATRP process mediated by light and catalyzed by an organic-based photoredox catalyst. By employing a highly reducing phenothiazine derivative, vinyl monomers can be efficiently activated and deactivated with light, providing exceptional control over molecular weight, polydispersity, and chain-end fidelity. This approach yields polymers for which the molecular weight is accurately determined by the initiator-to-monomer ratio, mirroring the behavior of conventional ATRP but without the need for residual metal removal. Furthermore, this method facilitates the easy synthesis of block copolymers and demonstrates significant synthetic versatility through its compatibility with other controlled radical processes. We anticipate that these organic photoredox catalysts will not only unlock new applications for controlled radical polymerizations in sensitive fields like biomaterials and microelectronics but also prove valuable in broader small-molecule and polymer chemistry.
